title: Foundations of Democracy: From Athens to America
subject: Social Studies
overview: Students trace the development of democratic ideals from ancient Athens through the Enlightenment to the founding of the United States, examining how these ideas continue to shape civic life today.
essential_questions: How did ancient ideas about democracy shape the founding of the United States?
Why do citizens have both rights and responsibilities in a democracy?
enduring_understandings: Democratic principles originated in ancient civilizations and evolved through centuries of political thought.
The U.S. Constitution reflects a deliberate effort to balance individual liberty with collective governance.
Active civic participation is essential for a functioning democracy.

daily_lessons.description: Introduce the concept of democracy through ancient Athens. Students compare Athenian direct democracy to modern representative democracy.
Explore Locke, Montesquieu, and Rousseau. Students analyze how Enlightenment ideas challenged the divine right of kings.

daily_lessons.topic: The Declaration of Independence — Close Read
daily_lessons.description: Primary source analysis of the Declaration. Students identify Enlightenment influence and evaluate Jefferson's arguments.

daily_lessons.topic: Designing a Government — The Constitutional Convention
daily_lessons.description: Simulate the Great Compromise. Students debate representation and practice the art of political compromise.

daily_lessons.topic: The Bill of Rights — Why Amendments Matter
daily_lessons.description: Students examine the first 10 amendments and connect them to real-world scenarios using case study cards.
Document-Based Question assessment: students analyze primary and secondary sources to argue whether civic participation is a right, a duty, or both.

summative: DBQ essay: 'Is civic participation a right, a duty, or both?'

required_materials: Primary source packet (Declaration excerpts, Federalist Papers selections)
Bill of Rights case study cards
